content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

My Docker image was three point two gigabytes. A teammate pushed the same application at ninety four megabytes. I checked the number twice, sure the pipeline had broken.
It had not broken. I was building images the way everyone builds images in their first year, and then never going back to question it.
That habit caught up with us during a traffic spike, when our autoscaler tried pulling that same bloated image onto three new nodes at once.
The pull took so long that new instances could not come up fast enough, requests started timing out, and we rolled back a release that had nothing wrong with the code itself.
The problem was never the application. It was the box we shipped it in.
That is the part nobody warns you about. You learn Docker once, copy a pattern from a tutorial or a coworker’s Dockerfile, and carry it for years without asking whether it still makes sense.
I did that for four years. Then I rebuilt every image my team owned from scratch, and almost none of my old habits survived.
The Base Image Was Doing All The Damage
For years I started every Dockerfile with something close to this.
